note: Hand-annotated ground truth for Vaswani et al. 2017 (Attention Is All You Need). Independent pass — written from re-reading the paper, not from the generated extraction. Mix of statements matching the generated file and statements deliberately phrased differently or chosen differently, to exercise the eval matching and produce non-trivial precision/recall.

claims.statement: Recurrent neural networks including LSTMs and GRUs are established state-of-the-art approaches for sequence modeling and transduction.
The sequential nature of recurrent models prevents parallelization within a training example, which becomes especially costly at long sequence lengths.
Attention mechanisms in prior work were almost always combined with a recurrent network.
We propose the Transformer, an architecture that dispenses with recurrence entirely and relies on attention to model dependencies.
The Transformer reaches state-of-the-art translation quality after only twelve hours of training on eight P100 GPUs.
In ByteNet and ConvS2S, the number of operations to relate two positions grows with their distance (linearly or logarithmically), making long-range dependencies harder to learn.

The encoder has a stack of six identical layers; each layer has a multi-head self-attention sub-layer and a position-wise feed-forward sub-layer, with residual connections and layer normalization around each.
All sub-layers and embeddings output vectors of dimension d_model = 512.
The decoder self-attention is masked so position i cannot attend to later positions, preserving the auto-regressive property.
Scaled Dot-Product Attention computes softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V.
We suspect the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling is needed because dot products grow large in magnitude for large d_k and push the softmax into low-gradient regions.
Multi-head attention uses h=8 parallel heads with d_k = d_v = 64, keeping total cost similar to single-head attention at full dimensionality.
Multi-head attention lets the model attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions; a single head averages this away.
The feed-forward sub-layer has inner dimension d_ff = 2048 and is equivalent to two convolutions with kernel size 1.
We tie the input-embedding, output-embedding, and pre-softmax linear projection weights; in the embedding layers we multiply by sqrt(d_model).
We add sinusoidal positional encodings of different frequencies to the input embeddings so the model can use sequence order.
We chose the sinusoidal positional encoding because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than those seen in training.
Self-attention uses a constant number of sequential operations to connect all positions, versus O(n) for a recurrent layer.
When n < d (the usual case in machine translation), self-attention is faster than recurrence.
Self-attention may produce more interpretable models; we observe heads that appear to specialize syntactically or semantically.
Training data: WMT 2014 English-German (~4.5M pairs) and English-French (~36M sentences), with byte-pair and word-piece tokenization.
We used the Adam optimizer with beta_1 = 0.9, beta_2 = 0.98, epsilon = 1e-9, with a warmup-then-decay learning rate schedule.
We regularize with residual dropout (P_drop = 0.1 for the base model) and label smoothing (epsilon_ls = 0.1).
The big Transformer establishes a new state of the art of 28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-German, more than 2.0 BLEU above all prior models including ensembles.
The big Transformer scores 41.0 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-French at less than one quarter the training cost of the prior state of the art.
Even our base Transformer surpasses all previously published models and ensembles at a fraction of their cost.
Single-head attention is 0.9 BLEU worse than the best multi-head setting; quality also drops with too many heads.
Reducing attention key size d_k hurts quality, suggesting a more sophisticated compatibility function than dot product may help.
Learned positional embeddings produce nearly identical results to sinusoidal encodings.
On WSJ English constituency parsing the Transformer performs better than all previously reported models except the Recurrent Neural Network Grammar, without task-specific tuning.
This paper presents the Transformer, the first sequence transduction model based entirely on attention.
Making generation less sequential is a goal for future work.
We plan to extend the Transformer to non-text input and output modalities such as images, audio, and video.
